Brandon Wu.their_reply:
"Like MFA, but the factors are people!" -- you should be a marketer :)

My previous company built a sudo plugin for sudo that did something similar (sudopair) - at least conceptually. One challenge we ran into was making sure that people who needed to sudo would be able to get timely approvals. This typically required some out of band ping on Slack to the approvers group. This slowed down response when emergency access was required (like an incident).

Your PuPI publishing flow is likely less "emergency" driven unless there is a vulnerability that they need quick response to for a patch or the like. But just something to consider. Where you might run into problems is timeouts or delays if approvals are not granted timely. If we are assuming that it is a non-time sensitive request (like submit a request to publish and the developer can walk away until it's approved by X people), then it might be an event or queuing system that can just hold the request until approvals are in to proceed. I'm unsure, but if it is a live web connection that is trapped, it might have some sort of timing issues with long delays on approvals.

As for bypassing the proxy, it seems like the proxy has to be a reverse proxy enforced at the destination (rather than the client). This is probably OK for a POC / research project, but this would likely have to be something that is implemented on the PyPI registry side in a real implementation. Perhaps expanding on how that might work in your paper similar to some of the MFA requirements for publishing new packages that can be enforced now.

When I think of other places it might apply, I think of sensitive transactions that don't necessarily have a time-bound sensitivity (or the timing is a little more flexible). Or, situations where groups of people are already available to respond. Perhaps these could be access to sensitive data stores like backups, access to centralized root mfa keys (like in a password vault), or account recovery options for web services.

Thea Galano Nudo.their_reply:
Hi Ian,
I think this is a good idea, and the PyPI example is the one that convinced me the most. If a maintainer account is compromised, then someone could push a bad package update that could affect many people who depend on it. So, this seems like a case where asking for another maintainer to approve is reasonable.
One thing I would be careful about is choosing use cases where the extra approval step is worth the inconvenience. There's always a tradeoff between security and convenience, and if the system slows people down too much, people may try to work around it. So, I think this works best for high-impact actions that don't happen constantly.
The bypass issue also seems important. If maintainers can still publish directly to PyPI, then the proxy only helps when people choose to use it. For the prototype, maybe the proxy could hold the publishing token, so the publish action would have to go through the approval flow. You could also mention that a real version would need support from PyPI or the package registry.
For the signature part, maybe the first version could be simple: each approver signs release details with package name, version, file hash, and destination. Then the proxy just checks whether enough people approved before publishing. More complicated threshold signatures could be a topic for later discussion.
Another thing I wondered about is timing. Most package releases probably do not need to happen immediately, but a critical security patch might. If approvals take too long, that could be frustrating or even risky. Maybe the publish request could be saved in a queue instead of keeping a live request open while waiting for people to approve.
I also thought the audit idea was useful. It might help if the release showed who approved it, or if there was some record of approvals that could be checked later. That connects to CHAINIAC paper, Proactive Software-Update Transparency via Collectively Signed Skipchains and Verified Builds, because it also talks about software updates, multiple signers, and keeping release history auditable. It is not the exact same thing, but it seems like useful related to your practicum.
I really think this is a strong practicum idea. I hope my feedback helps. I'd be interested to see what you decide to build for the prototype versus what you leave as future work. Good luck.
Thea Galano Nudo.my_response:
Thanks for the feedback!
I do think it's worth thinking about security vs convenience. I hear many non-cyber people at work complain about MFA which they themselves control, imagine if they needed an approver for every time they logged it, work may never get done.

Thanks for the link to the paper as well, I'll have to read it!
